items.question: Who uses covariance and correlation in machine learning?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers use them in EDA, feature selection, multicollinearity checks, covariance-based PCA, regularized linear models, and anomaly detection (e.g., Mahalanobis distance built from Σ).

items.question: What is the Pearson correlation coefficient?
items.answer: Pearson ρ (or sample r) is Cov(X, Y) divided by σ_X σ_Y, measuring linear association strength on a scale from -1 to +1, independent of positive linear rescaling of X or Y.

items.question: When should you report covariance instead of correlation?
items.answer: When the magnitude in original units matters—e.g., quantifying how many dollars revenue moves per dollar of ad spend—or when building covariance matrices for algorithms that expect Σ (PCA on unstandardized features, Gaussian models).

items.question: Where do covariance matrices appear in ML pipelines?
items.answer: In computing sample covariance from centered design matrices, PCA/SVD preprocessing, Gaussian likelihoods, portfolio/risk models, and anywhere pairwise second moments describe feature geometry.

items.question: Why standardize before comparing feature relationships across datasets?
items.answer: Covariance depends on units and scale; correlation removes marginal scale so you can compare which pairs are linearly linked without one high-variance feature dominating raw covariances.

items.question: How do you compute sample Pearson r from data vectors x and y?
items.answer: Center both vectors, compute s_XY = Σ(xᵢ - x̄)(yᵢ - ȳ)/(n - 1), divide by √(s_XX s_YY) where s_XX and s_YY are sample variances with the same n - 1 divisor—or use `np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]`.

items.question: Covariance vs Pearson correlation: what changes when you standardize?
items.answer: Covariance Cov(X, Y) is in units of X times units of Y and changes if you rescale a variable. Pearson ρ divides by σ_X σ_Y, is dimensionless, bounded in [-1, 1], and unchanged by positive linear scaling—so it compares linear strength across features on different scales.

items.question: Sample covariance with n vs n - 1 divisor: which matches `np.cov`?
items.answer: `np.cov` defaults to the unbiased estimator with divisor n - 1 (Bessel correction). A population-style divisor n yields a slightly smaller matrix; use n - 1 for sample covariance matching standard statistical texts and sklearn-style estimators.
